title: Sort in three ways

placement.where: replaces NCERT Ch 6 Activity 6.2 / Fig. 6.4 — students sort the same set of everyday objects three different ways (by shape, colour, then material) to discover that the same things can be grouped by different properties

windowMethods: setProperty(property) — switch the active sort pass; property is 'shape'|'colour'|'material'. Only switches to a not-yet-completed pass; changes which property placeCard/sort grades against.

studentControls.control: Tap an object in the tray to select it, then tap the matching property bin
studentControls.effect: if the object's property matches the bin it snaps in with a ✓ pop and the correct chime; a wrong bin shakes red, rejects, and the object stays in the tray

studentControls.control: Save & advance button (appears when every object in the step is placed)
studentControls.effect: marks the pass complete and auto-advances to the next property (shape → colour → material), or shows the result on the third pass
clears all three boards and returns to Step 1 (Shape)

answerKey.rule: A placement is correct only when tile[activeProperty] === binId. There is exactly one correct bin per object per pass, so the activity cannot be completed incorrectly. The colour pass omits the key (7 objects); shape and material use all 8.
answerKey.agentVerbNotes: placeCard / sort accept a SINGLE id or an ARRAY. Each entry is a tile id (string) or {id, bin}. When bin is omitted the card auto-places into its correct bin for the active property, so the agent can credit several objects at once, e.g. placeCard(['apple','straw','coin']) on the shape pass fills the Round bin. Ids and bins resolve by id OR by literal label (case-insensitive). setProperty('shape'|'colour'|'material') switches the active sort.
answerKey.operatorModeNote: ai = focused demo: the bins/boards (phenomenon) and read-out stay visible, the object tray + Save/Try-again buttons hide and student taps are inert (the agent drives via placeCard()/setProperty()/highlight()); student = full interactive controls. Default student.

scientificFacts: Classification means arranging objects into groups by a property they share, such as shape, colour or the material they are made of.
The same set of objects can be grouped in many different ways — sort by shape, then by colour, and the groups change completely.
Objects are made of materials; for example a coin and a key are metal, while a log and a wood block are wood.
Grouping things helps us notice patterns and find what we need more easily.
